industrial-ontario-ca.summary: The Ontario, CA industrial submarket (Inland Empire West) is in a post-peak recalibration phase. Direct vacancy has climbed to roughly 7.7% to 8.5% in early 2026, up sharply from sub-2% pandemic-era lows, as a wave of speculative construction delivered vacant space faster than demand could absorb it. Lease rates have fallen roughly 34% from their 2023 peak to around $0.99 to $1.08 per SF per month NNN, giving tenants meaningful negotiating leverage including free-rent concessions. Sale prices have pulled back to roughly $220 to $320 per SF depending on asset quality and size, with cap rates averaging in the mid-4% to low-5% range for stabilized product.
industrial-ontario-ca.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Ontario/Inland Empire West pulled back roughly 15% from 2022 peak levels through mid-2026, with the average transaction price settling in the $259-$275/SF range during 2025, compared to highs above $300/SF in 2022.
value_drivers: Rising vacancy (7.7%-8.5% direct in Q1 2026) driven by excess speculative deliveries is pushing lease rates and values down
Lease rates down roughly 34% from 2023 peak to ~$0.99-$1.08 NNN/SF/month, eroding NOI and limiting upward pricing pressure on sales
Scarcity of smaller sub-100,000 SF modern logistics product is keeping that size band more competitive than big-box
Proximity to Ontario International Airport and I-10/I-15/SR-60 interchange continues to support long-term logistics demand and prevents deeper value declines

industrial-dallas-tx.summary: The Dallas industrial market is one of the most active in the United States, with vacancy declining to 8.7-8.8% in Q1 2026 and average asking rents reaching approximately $10.24/SF NNN annually. Investment sale pricing averaged $143-147/SF in Q1 2026, with cap rates holding near 6.2% on stabilized assets. Demand from 3PL, e-commerce, and logistics users is outpacing new deliveries for the first time in several years, signaling a tightening leasing environment ahead, though elevated sublease availability continues to give tenants some negotiating leverage.
industrial-dallas-tx.market_trend: Dallas-Fort Worth industrial sale prices trended upward from an average of approximately $129/SF in Q4 2025 to $143-147/SF by Q1 2026, reflecting continued investor demand for stabilized logistics assets despite a more cautious transaction environment.
value_drivers: Vacancy declining 137 bps from peak to 8.7-8.8%, reducing tenant leverage in bulk logistics submarkets
Elevated sublease availability near 10.5% of market continues to suppress effective rents and compress pricing on value-add assets
Infill submarkets (South Stemmons, DFW Airport corridor) face land scarcity, pushing premium pricing well above metro averages
Record 2025 leasing volume of 56 million SF - 50% above long-term norms - driven by 3PL and e-commerce users supporting rent growth

industrial-atlanta-ga.summary: Atlanta's metro industrial market is in a gradual recovery after vacancy peaked near 9% in mid-2025. Vacancy has since declined to approximately 7.5-7.6% as of Q1 2026, driven by improved leasing absorption and a sharp pullback in new construction deliveries. Average sale prices for metro warehouses have ranged from roughly $90 to $179 per square foot according to CBRE and Colliers, with the all-market average hitting an all-time high of approximately $146 per square foot in Q4 2025. NNN asking rents have softened to around $8.81 per square foot annually but are expected to firm as the pipeline tightens further.
industrial-atlanta-ga.market_trend: Average industrial sale prices in metro Atlanta rose sharply to an all-time high of approximately $146 per square foot in Q4 2025, up 34.5% quarter-over-quarter, after ranging between $90 and $179 per square foot across 2024-2025 transactions per CBRE and Colliers.
value_drivers: Vacancy compression from 9.0% in mid-2025 to 7.5-7.6% by Q1 2026 is tightening available supply and supporting pricing
New construction deliveries have fallen sharply to an 11-year low in Q4 2025, constraining future supply and limiting rent-dilutive new product
Large-block mid-size space (100,000-500,000 SF) faces highest availability, making smaller infill assets relatively scarce and better priced
Tariff-related supply chain reshoring activity and sustained e-commerce demand from I-85 North and I-20 West submarkets are driving leasing velocity

industrial-phoenix-az.summary: The Phoenix metro industrial market is actively recovering after absorbing over 91 million SF of new supply delivered between 2023 and 2025. Vacancy has been declining for four consecutive quarters, reaching roughly 9.2% to 12.4% depending on the source and methodology, as Q1 2026 absorption of 4.8 to 5.0 million SF outpaced new deliveries of just 1.4 million SF by a 3-to-1 margin. Sale prices rose sharply in Q1 2026 to an average of $245 per SF, up 17% year-over-year, with the largest deals concentrated in Class A logistics buildings in the Northwest and Southwest corridors. Small-bay and infill industrial space remains the tightest segment of the market, with vacancy as low as 4 to 6%, while large-format logistics buildings face more competition from available sublease space.
industrial-phoenix-az.market_trend: Phoenix industrial sale prices rose approximately 17% year-over-year to an average of $245 per SF in Q1 2026, recovering from a 2024-2025 trough driven by record oversupply, with institutional capital re-engaging aggressively for stabilized Class A assets.
value_drivers: Vacancy compression: four consecutive quarters of declining vacancy as absorption outpaces sharply reduced new deliveries (down 82% YOY in Q1 2026)
Semiconductor and advanced manufacturing demand surge: TSMC, Amkor, and 60+ industry expansions since 2020 driving structural industrial demand independent of logistics cycle
Sharp bifurcation between small-bay (4-6% vacancy, rent growth) and large-format logistics (16%+ vacancy, oversupply still working through)
Tariff-driven metals inflation (steel up 20.7% YOY) and power/electrical gear lead times of 42-52 weeks constraining new construction starts and supporting existing asset values

industrial-columbus-oh.summary: The Columbus, OH industrial market has shown strong momentum over the past two years, with industrial vacancy falling sharply from 9.4% in Q1 2025 to 6.6% by Q1 2026 as demand significantly outpaced new supply. Investment sales volume reached $1.1 billion through the first nine months of 2025, the highest since 2022, driven by both institutional and private buyers. Average asking NNN lease rates hover around $9 to $10 per square foot, while sale prices across the market average roughly $95 to $155 per square foot depending on building class and submarket. Modern Class A bulk distribution facilities and Rickenbacker logistics corridor assets command significant premiums over general industrial product.
industrial-columbus-oh.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Columbus have firmed over the 2024-2026 window, with total investment volume reaching $1.1 billion through Q3 2025 (highest since 2022) and average per-square-foot pricing supported by institutional re-entry and record demand absorption.
value_drivers: Vacancy falling sharply from 9.4% in Q1 2025 to 6.6% in Q1 2026 as net absorption far exceeded new deliveries
Speculative construction pipeline at its smallest since 2019 with deliveries slowing 39% year-over-year, tightening available supply
Scarcity of modern Class A bulk distribution product, which commands rents 9% above general industrial and drives outsized sale prices along Rickenbacker logistics corridor
Growing manufacturing demand from Anduril Arsenal-1 and onshoring activity increasing competition for mid-size and flex industrial space

office-dallas-tx.summary: The Dallas office market is highly bifurcated: trophy and Class A Uptown and Preston Center assets attracted a surge of institutional capital in 2024-2026, with sale prices ranging from roughly $350/SF to over $747/SF for the best buildings, while overall metro vacancy remains elevated near 24-25% and Class B properties continue to post negative absorption. Asking rents hit a record high of $33-$34/SF full-service across the metro in early 2026, with Uptown commanding roughly $60/SF. The strongest leasing and investment activity is concentrated in Uptown, Preston Center, and the North Dallas Corridor, while the CBD and Las Colinas submarkets face ongoing headwinds from high vacancy and tenant consolidations.
office-dallas-tx.market_trend: Sale prices for Dallas office assets rose sharply for institutional-quality properties in 2025, with the two largest Uptown trades pricing at $639-$747/SF and total metro sales volume jumping from $107M in all of 2024 to $1.46B through August 2025, though distressed and Class B assets continued to trade at steep discounts or go to foreclosure.
value_drivers: Trophy and Class A office vacancy in Uptown and Preston Center tightened sharply, with Preston Center availability at just 6.8% in H1 2026, creating significant rent and pricing premiums over the metro average
Class B office posted 493,000 SF of negative net absorption in Q1 2026, widening the pricing gap between quality tiers and pushing commodity office cap rates above 7.5% to clear
No new office deliveries in Q1 2026 for the first time in 30 years, reducing supply-side pressure in submarkets with strong demand and supporting rent growth of 6% year-over-year
AT&T's planned exit of 2 million SF from Downtown Dallas by 2028 is expected to push CBD vacancy well above its current 33%, depressing values in that submarket

office-atlanta-ga.summary: The Atlanta office market remains a tale of two cities: premier Class A and Trophy buildings in Midtown and Buckhead are trading at $158 to $228 per square foot with strong leasing demand driven by a flight-to-quality trend, while the broader market averaged $131 per square foot in 2025, down slightly from 2024. Overall vacancy hovers near 21 to 27 percent depending on the source, but the construction pipeline is at its lowest level in over a decade, which is expected to tighten supply and support Class A rents going forward. Investment sales activity in 2025 surged 56 percent year-over-year, signaling returning buyer confidence, though many assets are still trading well below replacement cost. Tenants are consolidating into higher-quality spaces, leaving older Class B and C product with elevated vacancy and flat rental rates.
office-atlanta-ga.market_trend: Office sale prices in Atlanta declined modestly in 2025, with the average price per square foot falling 2.66% year-over-year to $131/SF broadly, though top-tier Midtown Class A assets traded at $158 to $228/SF, outperforming the market average as investors selectively targeted stabilized, well-leased buildings.
value_drivers: Flight-to-quality: 73.5% of new leasing activity concentrated in Trophy/Class A assets, leaving Class B/C with elevated vacancy and flat absorption
Supply scarcity: construction pipeline at lowest level since 2011, with only ~272,000 SF under construction metro-wide, reducing future supply pressure
Persistent high vacancy: overall market vacancy of 26-27% is keeping broad sale prices well below 2019 peaks and replacement cost
Return-to-office mandates: corporate RTO policies are supporting leasing demand in premium Midtown and Buckhead submarkets, pushing Class A asking rents above $40/SF

retail-phoenix-az.summary: The Phoenix retail market is performing strongly entering mid-2026, with vacancy holding near 5.0% and average asking rents reaching $26.57/SF in Q4 2025 after five-plus consecutive years of annual rent growth averaging around 5-6%. Investment activity has accelerated, with transaction volume in 2025 running more than 25% above 2024 levels and new-construction grocery-anchored centers trading above $650/SF while larger power centers clear in the $250-$270/SF range. Unanchored and non-grocery-anchored strip centers typically trade at 7-8% cap rates, with top-tier stabilized assets compressing into the 6% range. No specific building address was provided for the target property, so a subject building size could not be determined from public records.
retail-phoenix-az.market_trend: Phoenix retail sale prices have risen significantly over the 2024-2026 window, with new-construction stabilized assets trading at record per-SF levels above $650/SF and the broader market averaging $187/SF on large-format investment-grade product, supported by accelerating transaction volume that surpassed $2 billion in the trailing twelve months through Q3 2025.
value_drivers: Sustained sub-5% retail vacancy driving landlord pricing power and above-market rent escalations
New-construction grocery-anchored and daily-needs centers commanding $600+/SF as private all-cash buyers bypass debt markets
Over $600M in maturing CMBS retail loans by 2026 may push value-add inventory to market, creating acquisition opportunities
National chain bankruptcies (99 Cents Only, Joann) temporarily lifted availability to 5.1% but space is being rapidly backfilled by off-price, fitness, and experiential tenants

industrial-fontana-ca.summary: The Fontana industrial submarket within Inland Empire West remains one of Southern California's most active logistics corridors, anchored by major freeway access to I-10, I-15, I-210, and SR-60. Institutional buyers including Bridge Logistics Properties and Rexford Industrial closed multiple large transactions between late 2024 and early 2026, with stabilized Class A assets trading in the $250 to $265 per square foot range. The market's vacancy rate stood at approximately 8.4 percent at year-end 2025, slightly elevated versus prior years due to a wave of new completions, yet demand from large logistics users including Amazon continues to absorb supply. Average asking NNN rents for warehouse and distribution space in Fontana are approximately $17 per square foot annually.
industrial-fontana-ca.market_trend: Stabilized mid-size Class A industrial sale prices in Fontana held in the $250-$265 per square foot range from late 2024 through early 2026, while large vacant big-box assets traded at a discount near $158 per square foot, reflecting bifurcated pricing between leased and vacant product.
value_drivers: Scarcity of large-format Class A industrial sites due to AB 98 and land constraints limiting new development pipelines
Elevated vacancy in the 8-9% range for big-box product above 500,000 SF creating pricing dislocation for mega-scale assets
Sustained demand from e-commerce and 3PL tenants such as Amazon absorbing mid-size and large distribution space
Proximity to I-10, I-15, and Ontario International Airport sustaining rent premiums for well-located Fontana product

industrial-las-vegas-nv.summary: The Las Vegas industrial market is working through the tail end of a supply-driven correction, with vacancy at roughly 11.4% in Q1 2026 but absorption trending positive at 5.1 million SF in 2025. Sale prices show a wide spread, from about $152/SF for large institutional big-box product up to $290/SF for smaller Class A infill assets, with the Southwest submarket commanding a clear premium. Asking rents have stabilized around $14 to $16 NNN annually, and most well-priced industrial product is clearing with little to no concession. The spec construction pipeline has effectively paused, which should support gradual absorption of standing vacancy through the rest of 2026.
industrial-las-vegas-nv.market_trend: Industrial sale prices in Las Vegas have bifurcated over the past 24 months: small-bay infill assets in the Southwest submarket have held or edged higher ($250-$300/SF range), while large-format distribution properties have seen price compression to $140-$165/SF as buyer discovery works through elevated vacancy.
value_drivers: Vacancy rose from sub-5% lows to 11.4% in Q1 2026 due to 2023-2025 supply wave, compressing values on large-bay product
Spec groundbreakings paused broadly across the market, reducing near-term pipeline and supporting rent stability
Sublease shadow supply reached 2.6 million SF, creating direct competition with landlords for mid-size tenants
Small-bay infill product under 25,000 SF in Southwest Las Vegas remains scarce and trades at a 60% premium to the broader valley

industrial-savannah-ga.summary: The Savannah industrial market remains one of the strongest in the Southeast, driven by record Port of Savannah cargo volumes and proximity to the Hyundai EV manufacturing plant. Vacancy rose through much of 2024-2025 due to a wave of speculative deliveries but began stabilizing near 9.9%-14.1% by early 2026 as new construction slowed. Average sale prices for Class A product tracked around $99-$137 per square foot in 2024-2025, while NNN asking rents for functional warehouse space in the immediate Savannah submarket ranged from roughly $8.54 to $12.00 per square foot annually. Investment activity remains robust with institutional and private buyers active across the Highway 21 corridor and port-adjacent submarkets.
industrial-savannah-ga.market_trend: Average industrial sale prices in Savannah rose from approximately $85-$100 per square foot in early 2024 to $130-$133 per square foot by Q4 2025, reflecting continued institutional demand despite rising vacancy from new supply deliveries.
value_drivers: Port of Savannah cargo volume growth creating sustained logistics tenant demand along I-16 and I-95 corridors
Hyundai EV plant generating 8,100 projected jobs and new supplier demand for proximate industrial space
Elevated vacancy (9.9%-14.1%) from 2023-2025 speculative construction wave pressuring rent growth and lease-up timelines
Declining new development pipeline in 2025-2026 beginning to rebalance supply and support stabilization

office-austin-tx.summary: Austin's office market remains tenant-favorable in mid-2026, with a citywide vacancy rate near 24-25% - one of the highest among major U.S. metros - giving tenants strong negotiating leverage on rents and concessions. Closed sale prices in 2025 averaged around $216/SF across all classes, above the national average of $192/SF, though individual deals ranged from under $130/SF for value-add suburban assets to over $400/SF for trophy downtown towers. Lease rates for Class A space run roughly $45-$65/SF full-service in the CBD and $28-$45/SF in suburban submarkets. A shrinking construction pipeline and early return-to-office mandates from major tech employers point toward gradual tightening of conditions through 2026 and into 2027.
office-austin-tx.market_trend: Austin office sale prices declined from a 2024 average of ~$403/SF (inflated by the $521.8M Block 185 trophy trade) to a 2025 average of ~$216/SF as institutional sellers like Starwood exited at deep discounts and value-add buyers dominated deal flow.
value_drivers: Elevated overall vacancy (~24.6% as of early 2026) suppressing values and keeping buyers cautious
Shrinking new construction pipeline (down 91% quarter-over-quarter in Q3 2025) reducing future supply pressure
Return-to-office mandates from tech tenants (NVIDIA 230K SF expansion, NinjaOne HQ) absorbing sublease inventory
Texas SB 840 enabling office-to-residential conversions creating optionality and floor for distressed asset pricing
